skill.name: plan-approval
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through explicit plan creation, review, approval, revision, and deviation tracking before complex or risky coding work.
skill.summary: Require user approval before complex AI-assisted code changes.
skill.author: DojoCodingLabs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell-style backticks, weak-crypto keywords, reconnaissance words, and one environment-file reference. Manual review found these are Markdown templates, inline command examples, and illustrative file paths in a workflow guide, not executable code or secret access. No prompt-injection attempt, malware intent, data exfiltration, or hidden network behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Documentation Examples Trigger Static Security Patterns
low_findings.description: The flagged backticks and command names appear inside Markdown fences, inline examples, and command documentation for a plan-approval workflow. They do not execute commands, spawn processes, or fetch remote data, but users should understand that the skill recommends storing plan records under a local .claude memory directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Markdown templates or inline command names, and the repository contains only SKILL.md. There is no executable script or dynamic shell invocation in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Illustrative Environment File Reference
low_findings.description: A Stripe billing example lists .env.example as a file to modify with Stripe keys. This is documentation for an example plan, not code that reads secrets or transmits environment values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment reference appears in a quoted example plan under files to modify. It does not include credential values or code that accesses environment variables.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Fenced Markdown Backticks
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis interpreted Markdown code fences and inline backticks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The reviewed context shows they are presentation syntax for templates, file paths, and slash-command examples.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines are inside Markdown code fences or inline Markdown formatting. No runtime interpreter or script file exists in the skill package.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keywords
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak-cryptography findings are caused by ordinary words such as approval, approach, and risk in planning templates. No hash function, cipher, or cryptographic implementation is present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows generic planning language, not calls to MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar algorithms.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Terms in Workflow Text
dangerous_patterns.description: Scanner reconnaissance hits map to phrases about user review, plan review, and webhook examples. They do not instruct the assistant to enumerate hosts, scan networks, or collect system information.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines are normal approval-workflow prose. No commands such as port scanners, network probes, or system inventory collection are present.

content.user_title: Control AI Changes With Approval Plans
content.value_statement: AI assistants can move too quickly on complex or risky changes. This skill adds structured plans, explicit approval checkpoints, revision loops, and deviation tracking before execution.
actual_capabilities: Defines when AI assistants should request approval before coding work.
Provides minimal and comprehensive plan templates for proposed changes.
Describes approval, rejection, and modification response handling.
Guides tracking of plan execution status and deviations.
Documents plan storage patterns for approved, rejected, proposed, and completed plans.
Shows how to learn from repeated user approvals and rejections.
limitations: It is a workflow guide, not an executable approval system.
It does not enforce approvals at runtime without assistant cooperation.
It does not include scripts, validators, or storage automation.
Example commands are documentation and may need separate implementation.

use_cases.title: Review Risky Code Changes
use_cases.description: Create a clear plan before migrations, dependency updates, refactors, or file deletions.

use_cases.title: Control Agentic Workflows
use_cases.description: Require explicit approval before an assistant starts work that affects architecture or production behavior.

use_cases.title: Document Decision History
use_cases.description: Store approved, rejected, and revised plans so future work reflects user preferences.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small feature or fix.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a short approval plan before making this change. Include the files, reason, risks, and approval question.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Risky Change
prompt_templates.scenario: Database, dependency, deployment, or breaking API work.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a comprehensive approval plan for this risky change. Include alternatives, rollback steps, testing, and required user approval.

prompt_templates.title: Revise a Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: The user wants a different approach before implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Revise the current plan using my requested changes. Show what changed and ask for approval again before execution.

prompt_templates.title: Handle a Plan Deviation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow when implementation no longer matches the approved plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the approved plan with the needed deviation. Explain the impact, risks, and options, then wait for explicit approval.

output_examples.input: Add Stripe subscription billing to the app.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns a structured plan with files to create, database changes, alternatives, risks, tests, rollback steps, and an approval request.

output_examples.input: Modify the plan to use Stripe Checkout instead of Elements.
output_examples.output: The assistant updates the plan, notes the changed approach, explains the impact, and asks for approval before continuing.

output_examples.input: Continue the approved implementation, but use a new dependency.
output_examples.output: The assistant flags a deviation, explains why the dependency changes the approved scope, and waits for a new decision.

best_practices: Use explicit approval for database changes, breaking APIs, deployments, dependencies, and file deletions.
Include concrete files, risks, alternatives, tests, estimates, and rollback steps in each plan.
Track rejections and modifications so future plans reflect the user's preferred approach.
anti_patterns: Do not treat silence or vague agreement as approval.
Do not continue after a major deviation without presenting the change first.
Do not store sensitive credentials inside plan files or examples.

faq.question: Does this skill execute approval commands?
faq.answer: No. It describes workflow patterns and example commands. Separate tooling must implement any command behavior.

faq.question: When should approval be required?
faq.answer: Use approval for database changes, breaking changes, architecture shifts, dependency updates, deployments, file deletions, and broad refactors.

faq.question: Can a user modify a plan?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow treats modification requests as a reason to revise the plan and request approval again.

faq.question: Does it help with rollback planning?
faq.answer: Yes. The comprehensive template asks for rollback steps for risky changes.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is written for assistant workflows and is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code usage patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Manual review found documentation examples but no executable code, prompt injection attempt, or exfiltration behavior.
